content.full_text:
al
SUPERSEDED
Practices Commission
August 6,
1993
Honorable Jerry J.
Smith
Councilmember,
City of Barstow
220 East Mountain View Street
Barstow,
CA
92311
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—93—215
Dear Mr.
Smith:
V
This is in response to your request for advice regarding your
duties as a Barstow City Councilinember under the conflict-of—
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.’!
Since you are
not seeking advice about a specific decision before you, your
letter is being treated as a request for informal assistance.
2
!
In your initial request you were also seeking advice relative to a
fellow councilmember.
The request for that advice was verbally
withdrawn on June 28,
1993.
This letter also contains facts provided in our June 28,
1993
telephone conversation.
QUESTIONS
1.
May you vote to approve payment of utility and energy
bills owed to Southern California Edison?
2.
May you participate in decisions on street improvements
which may border or be near the transmission or distribution
facilities owned by Southern California Edison?
3.
May you participate in decisions on street improvements
which require the relocation of utilities?
1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000 et g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section
18329(c)(3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
1—93—215
Page Two
CONCLUSIONS
1.
You may vote to approve payment of the city’s monthly
utility and energy bills if the approval of payment of energy
bills is a solely ministerial action which does not involve any
discretion as to whether,
or how much,
Southern California Edison
will be paid.
2 and 3.
You may not participate in street improvement
decisions if the decisions will increase or decrease Southern
California Edison’s gross revenues by $1,000,000 or more,
increase
or decrease expenditures by $250,000 or increase or decrease
assets or liabilities by $1,000,000 or more.
FACTS
You are a retired employee of the Southern California Edison
Company (the “SCE”)
and receive a retirement pension.
In addition
to your pension you have a common stock interest in SCE valued at
approximately $240,000 from which you receive dividends.
The city’s utilities are serviced by SCE.
Each month the
council votes on the payment of the bills the city has incurred,
including payment of utility and energy bills for street lighting
or energy consumption, to SCE.
You indicated that the approximate
monthly SCE bill is $40,000.
In addition to the above,
the council from time to time
considers various street improvements which may be near a
transmission or distribution facility owned by SCE.
Such
improvements may involve approval to resurface streets,
add
lighting,
add curbs,
or require the relocation of utilities.
At
present, there is no specific decision before you regarding a
street improvement but wish guidance for future use.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in,
or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.
An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official, or on a member of the official’s immediate
family,
or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.


Advice File No.
1—93-215
Page Three
***
(C)
Any source of inpome, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
For purposes of this section,
indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a
public official,
or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly,
or beneficially a 10—percent interest
or greater.
Section 87103
The term “investments” means any financial interest in or
security issued by a business entity,
including but not limited to
common stock, preferred stock, rights, warrants,
options, debt
instruments and any partnership or other ownership interest owned
directly,
indirectly or beneficially by the public official or
member of his or her immediate family.
(Section 82034.)
The term “income” includes any payment received,
including
but not limited to any salary,
wage,
advance, dividend, interest,
rent, proceeds from any sale, gift or loan.
The Act specifically
excludes from the definition of “income” dividends, interest,
or
any other return on a security which is registered with the
Securities and Exchange Commission of the United States
-
Government,
as well as any payments received under a defined
benefit pension plan qualified under Internal Revenue Code Section
401(a).
(Section 82030.)
Applying the above definitions to your economic interests,
your common stock interest in SCE meets the definition of
“investments” as used in Section 87103,
however the dividends you
receive from that stock are not “income” as used in Section
87103(c)..
You did not provide any information on the retirement
funds you receive from SCE.
If your retirement payments qualify
as a defined pension fund under IRS Section 401(a), your
retirement payments do not meet the definition of “income.”
Accordingly,
you may not make, participate in making,
or
attempt to use your official position to influence a governmental
decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally,
on
SCE..


Advice File No.
1-93-215
Page Four
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)
In addition,
the foreseeable financial effect on your
investment interest must also be material to require
disqualification.
The test for materiality differs depending on
the specific circumstances of each decision.
For example,
where
an economic interest is directly before the city council,
the
effect is generally deemed to be material and disqualification is
required.
3
!
(Regulation 18702.1(a),
copy enclosed.)
When a business entity is not the subject of a decision,
Commission Regulation 18702.2 provides the test for determining
whether the indirect effect of a decision on a business entity is
material.
For SCE,
a Fortune 500 company,
the effect of a
decision is material if:
(1)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease to the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $250,000 or more,
except in the case of any
business entity listed in the most recently
published Fortune Magazine Directory of the 500
largest U.S.
industrial corporations or the 500
largest U.S.
nonindustrial corporations,
in which
case the increase or decrease in gross revenues
must be $1,000,000 or more;
or
(2)
The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $100,000 or more,
except in the case of any business entity listed in
the most recently published Fortune Magazine
Directory of the 500 largest U.S.
industrial
corporations or the
500 largest U.S.
nonindustrial
corporations,
in which case the increase or
decrease in expenditures must be $250,000 or more;
or
A source of income is directly before the city council when
the economic interest initiates the proceeding by filing an
application,
claim,
appeal,
or similar request,
or is a named
party in,
or the subject of,
the proceeding.
A person or business
entity is the subject of
a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance,
renewal,
approval,
denial or revocation of any license,
permit,
or other entitlement to,
or contract with,
the subject
person or business entity.
(Regulation 18702.1(b).)


Advice File No.
1-93-214
Page Five
(3)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $250,000 or more,
except in the case of any
business entity listed in the most recently
published Fortune Magazine Directory of the 500
largest U.S. industrial corporations or the 500
largest U.S. nonindustrial corporations,
in which
case the increase or decrease in assets or
liabilities must be $1,000,000 or more.
Regulation 18702.2
Accordingly,
you would not be permitted to participate in
decisions affecting SCE if the decision would have the monetary
effect on SCE as -described above.
question i
- Payment of Utility Bills
You indicated that on a biweekly basis, the city council
agenda provides for the approval of payments to
SCE for energy
consumption for street lighting and other electric service needs.
In 1984, the Commission,
in its Klein Advice Letter No.
A—84-
152,
advised that a city councilmember could approve payments to a
medical foundation when such approval was on the council’s consent
calendar and the council authorized payment to the foundation to
satisfy its contractual obligation.
The approval of the payment
was a solely ministerial act which did not involve any discretion
as to whether,
or how much,
the foundation would be paid.
Similarly,
if the approval of payments to
SCE is a matter on
the consent calendar where the city council has no discretion as
to whether,
or how much,
SCE will be paid,
such action to approve
payment to
SCE would be deemed a ministerial act.
Public
officials do not “make” government decisions when they engage in
ministerial acts.
(Regulation 18700(d) (1).)
However, if there is dispute over whether,
or how much,
SCE
will be paid,
such action is not a ministerial act and you would
be prohibited from participating in decisions to approve payment
of utility bills to SCE.
(Klein Advice Letter,
supra.)
questions
2 and 3.
— Street Improvements
From time to time,
agenda items require council vote for
street improvements,
including the cost of utility relocation.
You indicated that on occasion the street improvements may be near
SCE’s transmission or distribution facilities.
For such decisions
you would use the materiality thresholds in Regulation 18702.2
described above, to determine if you would be permitted to
participate in the particular decision.


File No.
1—93—215
Page Six
For example, if a street improvement decision provided for
upgraded lighting,
extended utility lines or some other factor
would could increase the amount of utility services purchased by
the city,
such an increase may provide additional revenues to SCE.
If that additional services amounted to an increase in gross
revenues of $1,000,000 or more to SCE, the street improvement
decision would have a material financial effect on SCE and you
would not be permitted to participate in that decision.
Another example would be a street improvement decision for a
street which bordered property owned by SCE,
such as a
transmission station.
Such property would be an asset of SCE.
If
the street improvement increased or decreased the value of SCE’s
asset by $1,000,000 or more, the decision would have a material
financial effect on SCE and you would not be permitted to
participate in that decision.
I hope this letter has provided some guidance to you.
Should
you have any specific decision before you which may have an affect
on your economic interests, please feel free to contact the
Commission’s Legal Division for assistance at
(916)
322—5901.
Sin erely,
Y’eanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
JN/JET/jt
